title: Time Series in pandas: Resampling and Rolling, for Real
learningObjectives: Convert a timestamp column into a proper DatetimeIndex
Resample irregular or high-frequency readings into a fixed interval (e.g. hourly averages from per-minute readings)
Compute a rolling window statistic using .rolling(), the pandas equivalent of the SQL window function lesson
Choose the correct resampling aggregation (mean vs. sum vs. last) for a given kind of reading
whyItMatters: SQL's window functions computed a rolling average one query at a time. pandas is where that same rolling/resampling logic becomes fast, flexible, and directly chainable into the feature engineering Mission 3's forecasting models need — resampling per-minute readings into clean hourly features is a required step before any model can use them.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's time-series feature layer — hourly-resampled, rolling-averaged demand and weather data, in the exact shape scikit-learn's forecasting models expect as input in Mission 3.

problem.summary: Irregular per-minute readings need to become clean, regular hourly features.
problem.content: Your readings arrive every minute, irregularly, but the forecasting models in Mission 3 need clean, regularly-spaced hourly features — not a raw per-minute stream.

motivation.summary: This continues the SQL window-functions lesson's motivation, now as a reusable Python object.
motivation.content: This is the direct continuation of the SQL window-functions lesson's motivation: you need the trend, not just a single summary number, but now as an actual reusable Python object you can pass straight into a model instead of a one-off query result.

theory.summary: resample() buckets time into fixed intervals; rolling() computes a sliding-window statistic.
theory.content: `pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])` converts a string column into real datetime values; setting it as the index (`df.set_index('timestamp')`) unlocks time-aware operations. `df.resample('1h').mean()` groups all readings within each hour and aggregates them — `.mean()` for something like temperature, `.sum()` for something like energy consumed, `.last()` for a point-in-time value like current price. `df['demand_kw'].rolling(window=3).mean()` computes a rolling 3-period average, the direct pandas equivalent of the SQL `OVER (ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)` window function.

visualExplanation.summary: Irregular points snap into hourly buckets under resample; rolling reuses the SQL sliding-window visual.
visualExplanation.content: Irregular per-minute points snap into regular hourly buckets under `resample()`, with a separate rolling-window diagram reusing the sliding-window visual from the SQL window-functions lesson, now applied directly to a pandas Series.

interactiveDemo.summary: Adjust the resample interval and rolling window size on a live noisy series.
interactiveDemo.content: Adjust the resample interval (15min/1h/1d) and the rolling window size on a live noisy demand series and watch both the resampled and rolling-average lines redraw.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: convert to datetime, resample, and compute a rolling average.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
df["timestamp"] = pd.to_datetime(df["timestamp"])
df = df.set_index("timestamp")

hourly = df["demand_kw"].resample("1h").mean()
rolling_avg = df["demand_kw"].rolling(window=3).mean()
```

independentCoding.summary: Resample weather data with the correct aggregation per column, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Resample a provided per-minute weather DataFrame into hourly averages for temperature and hourly sums for precipitation, choosing the correct aggregation for each column.

debugging.summary: Fix a resample using the wrong aggregation for a temperature column.
debugging.content: Code uses `.sum()` to resample a temperature column into hourly values, producing physically meaningless summed-temperature numbers instead of an hourly average. Identify why the aggregation choice was wrong and fix it.

reflection.summary: mean() vs. sum() is a decision about meaning, not just syntax.
reflection.content: Why is choosing `.mean()` vs. `.sum()` for a resample not just a syntax choice, but a decision about what the resulting number is even supposed to mean?

teachBack.summary: Explain how resample and the SQL rolling-average lesson relate but differ.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why `resample('1h').mean()` on demand data and the SQL window-functions lesson's rolling average are solving two related but genuinely different problems.

miniProject.summary: Build the hourly-resampled, rolling-averaged features Mission 3 will consume.
miniProject.content: Build the hourly-resampled, rolling-averaged demand and weather features that Mission 3's first forecasting lesson will directly consume.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the exact feature shape Mission 3's forecasting models expect.
portfolioEvidence.content: This is the exact feature shape scikit-learn's forecasting models will expect as input starting in Mission 3.

labs.title: Resample & Rolling
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise resampling multi-column weather data with per-column-appropriate aggregations and computing a rolling average, including a deliberately wrong aggregation to fix.

simulations.title: Resample & Rolling Visualizer
simulations.description: Adjustable resample interval and rolling window size applied live to a noisy demand series, redrawing both derived lines in real time.

flashcards.front: Why would you use .sum() instead of .mean() when resampling per-minute energy consumption into hourly values?
flashcards.back: Energy consumed is additive over time — the hourly total is the sum of the per-minute amounts, not their average, which would understate the actual energy used in that hour.

flashcards.front: What's the pandas equivalent of the SQL rolling-average window function from the SQL skill folder?
flashcards.back: Series.rolling(window=n).mean() — it computes a rolling average over the last n rows, per row, without collapsing the DataFrame, just like the SQL OVER (ROWS BETWEEN ...) clause.

rubric: Correctly identifies and fixes the wrong aggregation choice for a resampled column
Chooses mean/sum/last appropriately per column based on what the underlying quantity actually represents
Rolling average computation matches a hand-verified expected result for a small sample

references.title: pandas User Guide — Time series / date functionality: Resampling
